title: Classification and Control: Purity and Danger in Britain’s Asylum Regime
abstract: This article engages Douglas caution against an ‘unsociological view of cognition’ that fails to recognise classification as an epistemological issue. In so doing, it offers a conceptual framework for addressing this linkage in relation to British attempts to define asylum all-but out of existence. Taking Zetter’s account of the fragmentation of refugee status as a starting point, and outlining the relevance of civic stratification for his argument, the article addresses the shift from viewing asylum primarily as forced migration to a focus on irregular entry. This shift is considered with respect to the role of classification as a mode of control, Mary Douglas treatment of ‘purity and danger’, Britain’s deployment of deterrent policy and the significance of a supporting ‘moral’ frame.
